coredata.dc:title: On the mechanism of graphene quantum dot encapsulation by chitosan: A molecular dynamics study
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Hao
Shen, Xin
Dai, Junhao
Peng, Guoteng
Liang, Lijun
Shen, Jia-Wei
Zhang, Li
coredata.dc:description: The safe and effective encapsulation and scavenging of carbon nanomaterials is of great importance in bionanotechnology. Chitosan has been extensively studied as a scavenger due to its superior biocompatibility, biodegradability, and strong adsorption capacity. In this work, molecular dynamics simulation was employed to systematically investigate the interactions between chitosan and GQDs at the molecular level. Our simulation demonstrates that chitosan chains in a highly basic solution can rapidly aggregate due to intermolecular hydrophobic interaction and encapsulate GQDs more efficiently, while chitosan chains in a highly acidic solution are relatively rigid and aggregate with difficulty due to repulsive electrostatic interaction, leading to a lower GQD capture efficiency. Moreover, with an increase in pH from 5.9 to 7.1, the hydrophobic segment of a chitosan chain increases and encourages the transition of the chitosan chain from a dispersed state to a self-aggregated state; thus, GQDs can be better encapsulated in the network of aggregated chitosan clusters. These results suggest that the behavior and mechanism of GQD encapsulation is mainly affected by the GQD-chitosan interaction and chitosan self-aggregation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Graphene quantum dot
Molecular dynamics simulation
Interaction
Cover rate
Encapsulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanisms of encapsulation of graphene quantum dots by chitosan
Effect of pH value on the encapsulation of graphene quantum dots by chitosan

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The final complexed structures of 100 ns MD simulations of the encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan in highly basic and acidic solutions: (a) the 4NH2-8GQDs system; (b) the 4NH3 +-8GQDs system; (c) the 4NH2-16GQDs system; (d) the 4NH3 +-16GQDs system. Water molecules are omitted for clarity. GQD molecules are shown in the blue licorice model.

The cover rate (CR) of GQDs varies with their distance from chitosan chains in two extreme cases. The solid line represents the CR of GQDs encapsulated by chitosan in a highly basic solution, and the dotted line represents the CR of GQDs encapsulated by chitosan in a highly acidic solution.
Normalized density distribution of GQDs carbon atoms on the surface of chitosan with different ratios in two extreme cases: (a) N chitosan/N GQDs = 4:8 (b) N chitosan/N GQDs 4:16.
The radial distribution function between the COM of GQDs in (a) the 4NH2-8GQDs and 4NH3 +-8GQDs systems; (b) the 4NH2-16GQDs and 4NH3 +-16GQDs systems.
Solvent accessible surface area (SASA) of chitosan versus the simulation time in (a) the 4NH2-8GQDs and 4NH2-16GQDs systems; (b) the 4NH3 +-8GQDs and 4NH3 +-16GQDs systems.

Snapshots of chitosan/GQDs complexes with N chitosan/N GQDs = 4:8 at the end of 100 ns MD simulations at different pH values. (a) pH = 5.9 (CS-80%); (b) pH = 6.5 (CS-50%); (c) pH = 7.1 (CS-20%). GQDs molecules are shown in licorice model.

(a) The cover rate (CR) of GQDs varies with their distance from chitosan at different pH values (5.9, 6.5, and 7.1). (b) Solvent accessible surface area (SASA) of chitosan versus the simulation time at different pH values (5.9, 6.5, and 7.1).
The protonation degree of chitosan at different pH values.
The details of all simulated systems in this work.

head.ce:title: On the mechanism of graphene quantum dot encapsulation by chitosan: A molecular dynamics study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Automation & College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The safe and effective encapsulation and scavenging of carbon nanomaterials is of great importance in bionanotechnology. Chitosan has been extensively studied as a scavenger due to its superior biocompatibility, biodegradability, and strong adsorption capacity. In this work, molecular dynamics simulation was employed to systematically investigate the interactions between chitosan and GQDs at the molecular level. Our simulation demonstrates that chitosan chains in a highly basic solution can rapidly aggregate due to intermolecular hydrophobic interaction and encapsulate GQDs more efficiently, while chitosan chains in a highly acidic solution are relatively rigid and aggregate with difficulty due to repulsive electrostatic interaction, leading to a lower GQD capture efficiency. Moreover, with an increase in pH from 5.9 to 7.1, the hydrophobic segment of a chitosan chain increases and encourages the transition of the chitosan chain from a dispersed state to a self-aggregated state; thus, GQDs can be better encapsulated in the network of aggregated chitosan clusters. These results suggest that the behavior and mechanism of GQD encapsulation is mainly affected by the GQD-chitosan interaction and chitosan self-aggregation.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of nanotechnology in recent years has led to the widespread use of carbon nanomaterials in various fields. In industrial applications, for example, they have become indispensable components of catalysts [1], sensors [2], and photovoltaic devices; in the field of biomedicine, they have been widely used as drug/gene carriers, in immunoassays, and in interventional diagnosis and treatment [3–5]. Graphene, a new type of carbon nanomaterial, has been extensively studied in the past few years due to its unique electrical and mechanical properties. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a monoatomic thickness structure similar to that of graphene, ranging in size from 1 to 100 nm [6]. GQDs have unique optical properties and retain many of the electrical and mechanical properties of graphene, which means they have many applications in bioimaging and drug delivery [7–12]. For instance, Zhu et al. pointed out that GQDs could serve as excellent bioimaging agents [12]. Chen et al. reported that GQDs could be used in targeted fluorescence imaging for cancer screening, and in the tracking and monitoring of drug delivery [13]. Wang et al. found that GQDs can enhance the nuclear absorption of DOX and promote the efficiency of DNA cleavage [14], and also found that the pegylated GQDs had stronger drug loading capacity and could release DOX by adjusting the pH value. In addition, Ding et al. [15] studied the process of GQDs as a probe to monitor the delivery and release of anticancer drugs. It was found that GQDs released DOX after entering cells, and DOX interacted with tumor cells to make them apoptosis, and the inherent blue fluorescence of GQDs can track the process of drug carrier internalization by cells. However, because of their small size, these nanoparticles may stay in the organism for a long time, thus inducing cell damage and even apoptosis. In the past few years, the cytotoxicity of graphene and its derivatives has been extensively evaluated in both theoretical and experimental studies [16–20]. Tu et al. showed experimentally and theoretically that pristine graphene and graphene oxide nanosheets can induce the degradation of the inner and outer cell membranes of Escherichia coli and reduce their viability. Graphene nanosheets can penetrate into and extract large amounts of phospholipids from cell membranes because of strong dispersion interactions between graphene and lipid molecules [21]. Akhavan et al. found that GO nanowalls reduced by hydrazine were more toxic to bacteria than unreduced graphene oxide nanowalls [22]. Therefore, the removal of graphene and its derivatives is particularly important for the promotion of biological and environmental safety.

Chitosan (CS) is a natural macromolecular flocculant that has been proven effective in capturing proteins, nanoparticles, and heavy metal ions in water [23–25]; it also has excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability [26,27]. Zinchenko et al. found that DNA/chitosan complexes with appropriate ratios could scavenge various nanoparticles efficiently [28]. Some studies have shown that chitosan can effectively remove protein from dairy wastewater at pH 6.0 [25]. Recently, our group found that chitosan with positive charge (high protonated state) has more solvent accessible area, which is beneficial for the encapsulation of polynucleotide [29]. Javanbakht et al. [30] have shown that the synergistic effect of pH sensitive biopolymerized carboxymethylcellulose and chitosan can enhance the stability of drug dosage in a long time. It is found by Justin et al. that the preparation of biodegradable and conductive chitosan-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite microneedles can be used for small and large molecular weight therapeutic drugs [31]. Comparing the flocculating effect of chitosan with a synthetic macromolecule flocculant, Ganjidoust et al. showed that chitosan has a higher removal rate of total organic carbon in wastewater [32]. However, at the molecular level, the mechanisms of interaction between chitosan and nanoparticles, as well as its scavenging effects, are still not clearly understood. Therefore, it is of great interest to investigate in detail by molecular dynamics simulation the encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan and the associated underlying scavenging mechanisms.

With the help of molecular modelling technology and fast-growing computational power, molecular dynamics (MD) simulation has been widely applied to study the structure, thermodynamics, and dynamics properties etc. of complicated systems in recent years in the field of bionanotechnology [33–37]. Many interesting results were obtained and consistent with the results in experiment. For example, Borca et al. used MD simulation to study the structural changes of chitosan gel in water and vacuum and also the effect of different degrees of protonation on the structure of chitosan [38]. These simulation results show that chitosan can be used as a flocculant to coat and remove nanoparticles. Morrow et al. studied the effect of pH on chitosan self-assembly by MD simulation. It was proved that chitosan chains were liable to cross-link at higher pH levels [39]. Wang et al. used MD simulation to study the aggregation behavior and drug encapsulation process of salicylic acid functionalized chitosan (COS/SA), revealing the encapsulation mechanism of functionalized chitosan on nanoparticles [40]. In this study, a series of simulations were performed to investigate the adsorption and encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan and underlying scavenging mechanisms. Moreover, the interactions between chitosan and GQDs in various pH conditions at the molecular level were studied and discussed.

In this study, the structure of polyglucose with 10 units were obtained from LEaP module in the AMBER [41] program, and the initial structures of deacetylated chitosan with 10 units were generated by replacing all of the polyglucose unit –OH groups into –NH2 or –NH3 + groups (as shown in Fig. S1(a) and (b) in Support information) to mimic the different protonation states of chitosan chains in two extreme cases involving highly (1) basic and (2) acidic solutions, as in our previous study [42]. Then the structures of the chitosan units with different groups (–NH2 and –NH3 +) were optimized at the HF/6-31G+ level by Gaussian03 software [43]. After that, the force field parameters of the –OH group in glucose were replaced by the parameters (including the atom charges and the bonded and non-bonded parameters) of the –NH2 and –NH3 + groups. The partial charges were derived by the calculation of chitosan unit with different functional groups using the restrained electrostatic potential (RESP) method at the HF/6-31G+ level. The force field parameters of the chitosan units were obtained from the AMBER force field (GAFF) using AmberTools [44]. The parameters for the bonds, angles, and dihedral angles between the different units of chitosan were derived from the reference [45]. These chitosan force field parameters were successfully applied in the study of doxorubicin encapsulation and release by chitosan, as reported in our previous work [46]. The structure of GQD7 was used in this study, as in our recent studies [33,47,48]. The force field parameters of GQDs were also obtained from these previous studies.

In addition, in order to study the effect of pH values on the encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan, several pH values (5.9, 6.5, and 7.1) were selected to reflect normal and pathological sites of the human body. As the pKa valve of amine groups in chitosan is 6.5 [49], the degree of protonation of chitosan at different pH values was calculated according to Eq. (1) [50]:
(1)
y =
N protontated
N total
=
10
pK a
− pH
1 +
10
pK a
− pH
where N
protonated is the number of protonated sites and N
total is the number of –NH2 or –NH3 + groups of chitosan. Then three different sequences of chitosan were constructed according to the degree of protonation and pH value (see Table 1 ), and the structures of these chitosan (as shown in Fig. S1(c)) were obtained by the same method as described above.

In the chitosan/GQDs composite system, the length of the chitosan chain with 10 units was about 5 nm, and a series of systems were constructed according to two different ratios (4:8 and 4:16), expressing the number of chitosan chains (N chitosan) to the number of GQD molecules (N GQDs) in two extreme cases (highly basic and acidic). In the simulation systems studying the effect of the degree of protonation (pH value) on the interaction of GDQs with chitosan, the ratio of chitosan chains to GQDs was 4:8 (see Table 2 for details). Na+ ions were added into the solution to neutralize the systems. All initial structures were randomly generated by Packmol [51], and the water molecules were represented by the TIP3P [52] model in all systems. The GROMACS 5.0.4 package [53] was used to perform all MD simulations with a time step of 2 fs. The cutoff distance for the van der Waals (vdW) interaction was 1.2 nm. Particle mesh Ewald (PME) [54] summation was used to calculate the long range electrostatic interaction, with a cutoff of 1.2 nm for the separation of the direct and reciprocal space summations. All bond lengths were constrained using the LINCS algorithm [55] during simulation. All systems experienced 50,000 steps of energy minimization. The NPT ensemble was used in the simulation with a constant temperature of 300 K imposed by a velocity rescaling method [56] and 1 bar of pressure controlled by a Berendsen barostat with a time constant of 2 ps. The periodic boundary condition (PBC) was applied to all the systems. One hundred ns MD simulations for all systems were carried out. Molecular visualizations of the MD trajectories were carried out via visual molecular dynamics (VMD) software [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of encapsulation of graphene quantum dots by chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the final complexed structures of 100 ns MD simulations of the encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan with different ratios in highly basic and acidic solutions. It can be seen that chitosan chains in the highly basic solution have stronger self-aggregation behaviors, and that GQDs have stronger adsorption on chitosan chains, leading to a much stronger assembly of GQD-chitosan complexes (Fig. 1(a) and (c)). However, chitosan chains in the highly acidic solutions find it difficult to aggregate and disperse in the solution due to electrostatic repulsion. By contrast, GQDs aggregate easily due to strong π–π stacking interaction; therefore, it appears that GQDs are stacked together in relatively big clusters and are distributed around the chitosan chains (Fig. 1(b) and (d)). From Fig. 1, one can see that the overall encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan in the highly basic solution is much higher than in the acidic solution. This could be attributed to the high hydrophobicity of chitosan in the highly basic solution, which easily leads to the aggregation of multiple chitosan chains, while the hydrophobic GQDs tend to disperse around the hydrophobic chitosan sites with less aggregated state of themselves. By comparison, the fully protonated chitosan in the highly acidic solution is hydrophilic, and the rigidity of chitosan chains is quite high due to intramolecular and intermolecular electrostatic repulsion. These hydrophilic chains prefer to disperse in the solution, with adsorbed GQD clusters connecting these chains in some cases, as shown in Fig. 1(b) and (d). Therefore, on the basis of the results and discussion above, it can be said that the encapsulation efficiency of GQDs by chitosan in a highly basic solution is much higher than that in a highly acidic solution.

To compare the encapsulation efficiency of GQDs by chitosan in different systems, the cover rate (CR) was defined as the ratio of the number of carbon atom in GQDs within a certain distance d from a chitosan chain (N
c ) over the total number of carbon atoms in GQDs (N
t ), as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
CR
d
=
Nc
d
Nt
where Nc is the number of carbon atoms in GQDs within a certain distance d from a chitosan chain, and Nt is the total number of carbon atoms in GQDs.

The CR of GQDs versus the distance in highly basic and acidic solutions was calculated and displayed in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that the CR of each system increases with the increase in distance. More importantly, with same ratio of N chitosan/N GQDs, the CR of GQDs in the highly basic solution is much higher than that in the highly acidic solution, which indicates that the adsorption and encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan in the highly basic solution is much stronger than in the highly acidic solution. This result is consistent with the snapshot of complex structures displayed in Fig. 1. On the other hand, the CR of GQDs decreases significantly with the increase in the concentration (number) of GQD molecules if the concentration (number) of chitosan chains remains unchanged in both the basic and the acidic solutions. This may be attributed to two factors: (1) with the increase in the concentration of GQDs, the adsorption site for GQDs on the chitosan chain was saturated; (2) GQDs prefer to aggregate and form larger clusters due to the π–π stacking interaction with concentration increase.

To further understand the influence of different functional groups (–NH3 + and –NH2) of chitosan on the distribution of GQDs around chitosan, the normalized density of carbon atoms of GQDs on the surface of chitosan in two extreme cases was calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . It was found that the peak positions of carbon atoms on the surface of chitosan in both systems are around 0.5 nm, but the peak of the normalized density distribution of carbon atoms in the 4NH2-8GQDs system is larger than that in the 4NH3-8GQDs system (the peak values are 53.2 and 28.9, respectively), which indicates that GQDs adsorbed much better to the chitosan chain with –NH2 groups. This may be attributed to the higher affinity of GQDs to the –NH2 group than to the NH3 + group. Similar results can be observed in Fig. 3(b) showing increasing GQD concentrations. The above results further prove that chitosan chains in an unprotonated state have a higher affinity to GQDs and can capture more GQDs molecules. In addition, by comparing Fig. 3(a) and (b), it can be seen that an increase in concentration of GQDs can remarkably reduce their normalized density distribution around chitosan, which verifies that the GQDs captured by characteristic of saturation and self-aggregation. These results are consisting with the snapshots in Fig. 1 and the discussion of CR.

In order to better understand the distribution and self-aggregation behaviors of GQDs, the radial distribution function (RDF) between the center of mass (COM) of two GQD molecules in two extreme cases (highly basic and acidic solution) was calculated and shown in Fig. 4 . In the 4NH2-8GQDs system, the peak position of RDF is about 0.47 nm, and it is within the typical range of the π–π stacking distance. In the 4NH3 +-8GQDs system, although the peak position is similar to that in the 4NH2-8GQDs system, the peak value is about 68 and much higher than the peak value of 46 in the 4NH2-8GQDs system. In addition, the shape of the RDF peak in the 4NH3 +-8GQDs system is sharper than that in the 4NH2-8GQDs system, indicating that the self-aggregation behavior of GQDs is more obvious in highly acidic solutions (4NH3 +-8GQD system). Similar results can be found in the 4NH2-16GQD and 4NH3 +-16GQD systems, as shown in Fig. 4(b). These phenomena arise from the strong adsorption of GQDs on unprotonated chitosan (in the 4NH2-8GQDs and 4NH2-16GQD systems) and the strong self-aggregation of unprotonated chitosan in highly basic solutions. GQDs can disperse into the network of unprotonated chitosan chains, stabilizing the GQD-chitosan complexes and enhancing the chitosan clusters with glue-like connections between different unprotonated chitosan chains. The hydrophobic interaction between GQDs and unprotonated chitosan thus partially eliminates the self-aggregation of GQDs. At the same time, the interaction between hydrophobic GQDs and hydrophilic protonated chitosan is relatively weak, and protonated chitosan chains can only assemble into clusters with difficulty, leading to a stronger aggregation of GQDs themselves. In addition, compared with the RDF in the 4NH2-8GQDs and 4NH2-16GQDs (or 4NH3+-8GQDs and 4NH3 +-16GQDs) systems, one can find that increasing the concentration of GQDs leads to stronger self-aggregation of GQDs (see Fig. 1). This result may be caused by an insufficient number of adsorption sites in chitosan as the concentration of GQDs increases.

As the aggregation of chitosan chains is one of the main factors affecting the encapsulation of GQDs, changes in the solvent accessible surface area (SASA) of chitosan chains during the simulations in highly basic and acidic solutions were calculated and shown in Fig. 5 . It was found that the SASA of unprotonated chitosan decreases rapidly in the first 10 ns in both the 4NH2-8GQDs and 4NH2-16GQDs systems, while the SASA of protonated chitosan in both the 4NH3 +-8GQDs and 4NH3 +-16GQDs systems hardly changes during the simulations, particularly at the end of the simulations. It further confirms that unprotonated chitosan can rapidly aggregate due to intermolecular hydrophobic interaction and encapsulate GQDs more effectively, while protonated chitosan is relatively rigid and aggregates with difficulty due to repulsive electrostatic interaction, leading to lower efficiency in the capture of GQDs, as discussed above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH value on the encapsulation of graphene quantum dots by chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: In Section 3.1, the mechanism by which GDQs are encapsulated by chitosan was investigated. However, it was only considered in highly basic and acidic solutions, and all the deacetylated chitosan units were either unprotonated or protonated in these extreme cases. Herein the structure of deacetylated chitosan with different degree of protonation (in the solution of pH = 5.9, 6.5, and 7.1) were constructed according to Eq. (1). The details of these systems can be found in Table 1. Fig. 6 shows the final complexed structures of 100 ns MD simulations of GDQ encapsulation by chitosan with N chitosan/N GQDs = 4:8 in the solution of pH = 5.9, 6.5, and 7.1. From the snapshots, one can see that with the decrease in the degree of protonation of chitosan (the increase in pH value from 5.9 to 7.1), the hydrophobic segment of the chitosan chain increases, which encourages the transition of the chitosan chain from a dispersed state to self-aggregated state. Moreover, at pH 5.9, the aggregation of GQDs is much more obvious, while at pH 7.1 GQDs are uniformly dispersed around chitosan, and some of them are encapsulated in the network of aggregated chitosan clusters.

The encapsulation efficiency of GQDs by chitosan at different pH values was quantitatively evaluated by CR, as shown in Fig. 7(a). It can be seen that the CR of GQDs on the surface of chitosan in the CS-20%-8GQDs system (pH = 7.1) is as high as 98% at 5 Å, and that it is 86% and 74% in the CS-50%-8GQDs (pH = 6.5) and CS-80%-8GQDs (pH = 5.9) systems, respectively. Therefore, as the protonation degree of chitosan decreases (and the pH value increases), the CR of GQDs significantly increases, which is consistent with the snapshots in Fig. 6.

The normalized densities of GQD carbon atoms on the surface of chitosan in various pH conditions are displayed in Fig. S2. The position of the maximum distribution in all three systems is around 0.5 nm, but the peak values of the distribution are highest in the CS-20%-8GQDs system (pH = 7.1) and lowest in the CS-80%-8GQDs system (pH = 5.9). This indicates that if the protonation degree of chitosan is low, GQDs tend to adsorb on chitosan chains due to relatively stronger interaction between the –NH2 group and GQDs, and that with the increase in the protonation degree, the affinity of GQDs to –NH3 + group is reduced, which leads to a lower distribution of GQDs around the chitosan chain. These results are consistent with the complex structures in Fig. 6 and the CR calculations in Fig. 7(a), indicating that in neutral solutions (pH = 7.1), chitosan exhibits superior encapsulation efficiency and ability to capture GQDs than in acidic solutions (pH = 6.5 and 5.9), which may result in better scavenging of GQD nanoparticles.

To further analyze the effect of the protonation degree on the aggregation of chitosan chains, the changes in the SASA of chitosan chains with simulation times at different pH conditions were calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. 7(b). It was found that the SASA of chitosan in the CS-80%-8GQDs system (pH = 5.9) fluctuates little and hardly changes during the simulation. This is because 80% of the chitosan units are protonated; thus, intramolecular electrostatic repulsion between the units makes the chain rigid, and intermolecular electrostatic repulsion between different chains disperses chitosan in the solutions. Fig. S3 displays the average end to end distance of chitosan chains during last 50 ns at different pH values (5.9, 6.5, and 7.1) and the probability distributions of end to end distance, confirming that the rigidity of chitosan chain increase with the increase of pH values.

As the degree of chitosan protonation reduces, the SASA decreases with the simulation time, indicating the aggregation of chitosan chains and reduced surface to solvent, meanwhile, the number of hydrogen bond among chitosan molecules increased, suggesting stronger aggregation effect of chitosan at higher pH values, as shown in Fig. S4. The conformation of chitosan chains also has a certain effect on GQD encapsulation. With more compact clusters, for example, in the solution of pH = 7.1, the network of aggregated chitosan could encapsulate more GQD molecules and partially eliminate the assembly of GQDs themselves.

From the above analysis and discussion, it can be concluded that chitosan can capture more free GQD molecules in neutral and basic solutions. The behavior and mechanism of GQD encapsulation is mainly affected by the GQD-chitosan interaction and chitosan self-aggregation. More interestingly, as GQDs have been considered an excellent potential drug carrier in many research studies in recent years, the encapsulation of GQDs by chitosan in neutral solutions and the release of GQDs from chitosan in acid environments (for example, the pH in tumor tissues and cells ranging from 5.0 to 6.8) makes the chitosan-GQD complex a potential delivery system for controlled anti-cancer drug delivery. A recent research indicates that the pH-induced protonation/deprotonation of the –NH2 groups on CS chains can result in a pH-dependent drug delivery behavior of the GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel, which is well consist with our results and show potential application of chitosan-GQD complex in drug delivery systems [30]. Although the model in this work is relatively simple, and in the context of flexible weak polyelectrolytes the effect of charge regulation could be important in some cases [58,59], it is the very early stage of investigating chitosan-GQD interaction under different pH values by molecular simulation. The model with more protonation states even in same pH value will be considered in our future work to understand the effect of charge regulation.

In this study, MD simulation was undertaken to investigate the effects of chitosan chains on the encapsulation of GQDs and the underlying interaction mechanism between GQDs and chitosan. It was found that chitosan chains in highly basic solutions can rapidly aggregate due to intermolecular hydrophobic interaction and encapsulate GQDs more efficiently, while chitosan chains in highly acidic solutions are relatively rigid and aggregate with difficulty due to repulsive electrostatic interaction, leading to a lower GQD capture efficiency, which was proved by the analysis of CR, RDF, and SASA. In addition, with a decrease in the protonation degree of chitosan (increase in pH value from 5.9 to 7.1), the hydrophobic segment of chitosan chains increases, which encourages the transition of chitosan chains from a dispersed state to a self-aggregated state, and chitosan exhibits better encapsulation efficiency and ability to capture GQDs. Therefore, chitosan can capture more free GQD molecules in neutral and basic solutions. These results suggest that the behavior and mechanism of GQD encapsulation is mainly affected by the GQD-chitosan interaction and chitosan self-aggregation. This study may improve understanding of the underlying mechanism of GQD encapsulation by chitosan and promote the design and application of chitosan-GQD-based biomedical devices for nanoparticle scavenging and controlled drug delivery.

Hao Ren: Investigation, Software, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Xin Shen: Investigation, Software, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Junhao Dai: Formal analysis. Guoteng Peng: Investigation, Software. Lijun Liang: Conceptualization. Jia-Wei Shen: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Li Zhang: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21674032, 21978060, 21978274). This work was also supported by Key Fostering Project of Scientific Research of Hangzhou Normal University (2018PYXML006), the High Level Returned Overseas Chinese Innovation Projects in Hangzhou and the start funding of Hangzhou Dianzi University (Nos. KYS195618111).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.113453.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ikeda, S. Ishino, T. Harada, N. Okamoto, T. Sakata, H. Mori, S. Kuwabata, T. Torimoto, M. Matsumura, Angew. Chem. Int. Edit 118 (2006) 7221–7224.
